answers:
**1. What happens for an existing clinic that has never opened this setting?**
Reminders **are sent automatically**. The decision lives in `auto_send_reminders_enabled` at `backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`:
```python
def auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):
    if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:
        return True
    return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))
```
Line 11-12: when the key is missing from `tenant.settings` (i.e. `None`), the function returns `True`. This is consumed at `backend/app/jobs/reminders.py:25` (`if auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):`), which then sends both an SMS and an email for every upcoming appointment (lines 26-30). All three seed tenants in `seeds/tenants.json` lack an `auto_send_reminders` key, so under current code every one of them would be enrolled the moment this job runs.

**2. Is that default the right behavior?**
No. This falls squarely in the skill's "anything that can trigger outbound messaging... invert the default: null/missing/malformed means off" category — reminders send real SMS/email to patients on the clinic's behalf. Defaulting to on silently enrolls every existing clinic (and every future clinic that just hasn't visited settings yet) into automated outbound patient communication they never opted into, with no admin action taken. That's a meaningfully different risk profile than `compact_calendar_view_enabled`, which is a pure display preference where defaulting on is low-cost if wrong.

There's also a second, independent bug worth flagging: the settings UI at `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7` renders `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}` — for a clinic with no `auto_send_reminders` key, `!!undefined` is `false`, so the checkbox displays **unchecked/off**. Meanwhile the backend resolver treats the same missing value as **on**. A clinic admin who opens settings and sees an unchecked box has no way to know the system is already sending reminders. This mismatch is itself disqualifying regardless of which default is "correct" — UI and backend must agree.

I would change `backend/app/feature_flags.py:11-12` to invert the null case to `False`:
```python
def auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):
    if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:
        return False
    return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))
```
That also resolves the UI mismatch for free, since the checkbox already renders unchecked for missing values — an admin who wants the feature would now have to explicitly check it, and their observed UI state and the actual backend behavior would finally match.

**3. How was this arrived at, and was that a sound basis?**
Git history is direct evidence of a copy-paste-without-re-deciding: `git show 25509a7` (SLOT-190, 2026-06-10) added `compact_calendar_view_enabled` alone, a legitimate default-on convenience toggle. `git show aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214, 2026-07-01) then added `auto_send_reminders_enabled` directly beneath it, with the identical `if ... is None: return True` structure — same shape, same default, no accompanying rationale. The SLOT-214 commit touched `feature_flags.py`, `jobs/reminders.py`, and `ClinicSettings.tsx` but did not touch `tests/test_feature_flags.py` at all — there is zero test coverage of `auto_send_reminders_enabled`'s default behavior, so nothing would have caught this if it had been re-evaluated and gotten wrong, and nothing forced anyone to think about the null case explicitly. This is exactly the anti-pattern the skill calls out by name: "Never copy-paste one resolver's null-handling into a new flag in the other category without re-deciding this explicitly." It was not a sound basis — the default here appears to have been inherited mechanically from an unrelated, lower-risk flag rather than chosen for this flag's actual blast radius.

**4. Before anyone enables this feature, what would you verify or change?**
- Invert the null/missing default to `False` in `feature_flags.py:11-12` (above), and add tests in `tests/test_feature_flags.py` mirroring the calendar tests but asserting `auto_send_reminders_enabled` is `False` for `settings={}` and for explicit `False`, and `True` only for explicit `True`.
- Confirm the UI and backend now agree (they will, once the default flips) — add a note/small test if the codebase has any UI test scaffolding, since this mismatch was invisible without reading both files side by side.
- Since flipping the default from on to off is itself a behavior change for any clinic that already relied on (or was silently getting) reminders, decide explicitly whether existing clinics need a one-time backfill/communication ("reminders are now available — opt in here") rather than a silent flip in either direction.
- Confirm there is no env-level master switch anywhere — I found none in `infra/main.tf` (only an `environment` variable) or elsewhere in this repo. Per the skill's flag recipe, add an env-level master gate ANDed with the per-tenant value, and document a rollback affordance (e.g., an env var that force-disables the job) before this goes live, since right now the only kill switch would be editing each tenant's JSONB row directly.
- Verify how/where `reminders.run()` will actually be invoked in production (cron, worker queue, etc.) — I found no scheduler wiring in this repo at all, so this job is currently inert; whatever infra actually schedules it (likely outside this repo) needs to be checked before assuming today's blast radius is zero.
- Double-check `seeds/tenants.json` is only a local/dev fixture and not mistaken for the real tenant table — it only has 3 rows and none has the flag set either way, which is a small sample; the real production `tenant.settings` data should be queried to see how many real clinics are currently unset.
report:
## Plan
I set out to answer four questions about the SLOT-214 automatic-reminder toggle in the `slotwise` repo: (1) what the current default behavior is for clinics that never touched the setting, (2) whether that default is sound, (3) how the code arrived at that default via git history, and (4) what to verify/change before enabling the feature. The `config-and-secrets-hygiene` skill's "Per-tenant flags: the null/missing semantics is a product decision" section directly frames questions 1–3, so I read it first and used it as the lens for the review.

## Actions
- Read `.claude/skills/config-and-secrets-hygiene/SKILL.md` in full — this shaped the whole review; its per-tenant-flag section states outbound-messaging flags must invert-default to off on null/missing, and warns against copy-pasting one resolver's null-handling into a different risk category.
- `find` at repo root → located the actual project at `slotwise/` (there are many unrelated sibling repos in this working directory: clinic-notes, cedarline, meadowdesk, medibill, etc.).
- Read `slotwise/backend/app/feature_flags.py` — contains `compact_calendar_view_enabled` (lines 4–7) and `auto_send_reminders_enabled` (lines 10–13).
- Read `slotwise/backend/app/jobs/reminders.py` — the batch job that consumes the flag.
- `git log --oneline --all -- backend/app/feature_flags.py` and `-- backend/app/jobs/reminders.py` → two commits: `25509a7` (SLOT-190) and `aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214).
- `git show 25509a7 -- backend/app/feature_flags.py` → shows the original file, with only `compact_calendar_view_enabled`.
- `git show aa4cf8d` (full diff) → shows SLOT-214 added `auto_send_reminders_enabled`, `reminders.py`, and `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx` in one commit.
- Read `slotwise/backend/app/messaging_client.py` — confirms `send_sms`/`send_email` are the actual outbound-message calls (currently stubbed to record in-memory, not a real transport, but that's what the job calls).
- Read `slotwise/seeds/tenants.json` — three seed tenants; none has an `auto_send_reminders` key.
- Read `slotwise/tests/test_feature_flags.py` and `git log -p --all -- tests/test_feature_flags.py` — only 2 tests exist, both for `compact_calendar_view_enabled`; the SLOT-214 commit did not touch this file at all.
- Read `slotwise/frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx` — the settings UI checkbox.
- `grep -rn "reminders.run|jobs.reminders|from backend.app.jobs"` and `find` over the whole repo → no scheduler/cron/infra wiring calls `reminders.run()` anywhere in this repo.
- Read `slotwise/infra/main.tf` and `slotwise/README.md` for context — `main.tf` only declares an `environment` variable, no job scheduling.

## Answers

**1. What happens for an existing clinic that has never opened this setting?**
Reminders **are sent automatically**. The decision lives in `auto_send_reminders_enabled` at `backend/app/feature_flags.py:10-13`:
```python
def auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):
    if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:
        return True
    return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))
```
Line 11-12: when the key is missing from `tenant.settings` (i.e. `None`), the function returns `True`. This is consumed at `backend/app/jobs/reminders.py:25` (`if auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):`), which then sends both an SMS and an email for every upcoming appointment (lines 26-30). All three seed tenants in `seeds/tenants.json` lack an `auto_send_reminders` key, so under current code every one of them would be enrolled the moment this job runs.

**2. Is that default the right behavior?**
No. This falls squarely in the skill's "anything that can trigger outbound messaging... invert the default: null/missing/malformed means off" category — reminders send real SMS/email to patients on the clinic's behalf. Defaulting to on silently enrolls every existing clinic (and every future clinic that just hasn't visited settings yet) into automated outbound patient communication they never opted into, with no admin action taken. That's a meaningfully different risk profile than `compact_calendar_view_enabled`, which is a pure display preference where defaulting on is low-cost if wrong.

There's also a second, independent bug worth flagging: the settings UI at `frontend/src/pages/ClinicSettings.tsx:7` renders `checked={!!settings.auto_send_reminders}` — for a clinic with no `auto_send_reminders` key, `!!undefined` is `false`, so the checkbox displays **unchecked/off**. Meanwhile the backend resolver treats the same missing value as **on**. A clinic admin who opens settings and sees an unchecked box has no way to know the system is already sending reminders. This mismatch is itself disqualifying regardless of which default is "correct" — UI and backend must agree.

I would change `backend/app/feature_flags.py:11-12` to invert the null case to `False`:
```python
def auto_send_reminders_enabled(tenant):
    if tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders") is None:
        return False
    return bool(tenant.settings.get("auto_send_reminders"))
```
That also resolves the UI mismatch for free, since the checkbox already renders unchecked for missing values — an admin who wants the feature would now have to explicitly check it, and their observed UI state and the actual backend behavior would finally match.

**3. How was this arrived at, and was that a sound basis?**
Git history is direct evidence of a copy-paste-without-re-deciding: `git show 25509a7` (SLOT-190, 2026-06-10) added `compact_calendar_view_enabled` alone, a legitimate default-on convenience toggle. `git show aa4cf8d` (SLOT-214, 2026-07-01) then added `auto_send_reminders_enabled` directly beneath it, with the identical `if ... is None: return True` structure — same shape, same default, no accompanying rationale. The SLOT-214 commit touched `feature_flags.py`, `jobs/reminders.py`, and `ClinicSettings.tsx` but did not touch `tests/test_feature_flags.py` at all — there is zero test coverage of `auto_send_reminders_enabled`'s default behavior, so nothing would have caught this if it had been re-evaluated and gotten wrong, and nothing forced anyone to think about the null case explicitly. This is exactly the anti-pattern the skill calls out by name: "Never copy-paste one resolver's null-handling into a new flag in the other category without re-deciding this explicitly." It was not a sound basis — the default here appears to have been inherited mechanically from an unrelated, lower-risk flag rather than chosen for this flag's actual blast radius.

**4. Before anyone enables this feature, what would you verify or change?**
- Invert the null/missing default to `False` in `feature_flags.py:11-12` (above), and add tests in `tests/test_feature_flags.py` mirroring the calendar tests but asserting `auto_send_reminders_enabled` is `False` for `settings={}` and for explicit `False`, and `True` only for explicit `True`.
- Confirm the UI and backend now agree (they will, once the default flips) — add a note/small test if the codebase has any UI test scaffolding, since this mismatch was invisible without reading both files side by side.
- Since flipping the default from on to off is itself a behavior change for any clinic that already relied on (or was silently getting) reminders, decide explicitly whether existing clinics need a one-time backfill/communication ("reminders are now available — opt in here") rather than a silent flip in either direction.
- Confirm there is no env-level master switch anywhere — I found none in `infra/main.tf` (only an `environment` variable) or elsewhere in this repo. Per the skill's flag recipe, add an env-level master gate ANDed with the per-tenant value, and document a rollback affordance (e.g., an env var that force-disables the job) before this goes live, since right now the only kill switch would be editing each tenant's JSONB row directly.
- Verify how/where `reminders.run()` will actually be invoked in production (cron, worker queue, etc.) — I found no scheduler wiring in this repo at all, so this job is currently inert; whatever infra actually schedules it (likely outside this repo) needs to be checked before assuming today's blast radius is zero.
- Double-check `seeds/tenants.json` is only a local/dev fixture and not mistaken for the real tenant table — it only has 3 rows and none has the flag set either way, which is a small sample; the real production `tenant.settings` data should be queried to see how many real clinics are currently unset.

## Next steps
If I were allowed to make changes, I would: (1) edit `feature_flags.py` to invert the default as shown above, (2) add the missing unit tests for `auto_send_reminders_enabled`, (3) grep the deploy/infra tooling (outside this repo, if it exists) for wherever `reminders.run` gets scheduled and add an env-level master flag gating it, and (4) flag the UI/backend default mismatch as a blocking review comment on the SLOT-214 change even independent of the default-direction fix.

## Confidence and gaps
High confidence on questions 1–3: each claim is backed by an exact file/line I read and a git commit I inspected directly (`aa4cf8d`, `25509a7`), not inference. Question 2's recommendation follows directly from the project's own skill guidance for this exact scenario (outbound messaging + per-tenant null semantics), so I'm confident in the direction of the fix.

Gaps: I could not verify production tenant data (only saw the 3-row `seeds/tenants.json` fixture, which may not reflect real clinic counts or existing opt-in state) or any scheduler/cron configuration that might live outside this repository — so I can't confirm today's actual blast radius if the job were triggered right now, only that nothing in this repo currently wires it up. I also did not find any consent-capture or compliance-review artifacts for SMS reminders in the repo; that may exist elsewhere but wasn't visible here.
